title: Performance Tuning & Profiling
description: Performance tuning is the art of identifying and eliminating bottlenecks. Linux provides powerful tools — perf, flamegraphs, ftrace, and BPF — for profiling CPU, memory, I/O, and network. At Enterprise, performance engineering is a dedicated role, and every infrastructure engineer should be able to use perf to find the bottleneck in any system.

sections.title: perf: The Universal Profiler
sections.description: The perf tool (part of linux-tools-common) uses hardware performance counters and kernel tracepoints to profile system performance with minimal overhead. perf can answer: 'Where is my program spending CPU time?', 'Why are there cache misses?', 'What is the kernel doing right now?' At Enterprise, perf is the first tool reached for CPU performance investigations.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Install perf ===
sudo apt install linux-tools-common linux-tools-$(uname -r)

# === CPU sampling ===
# Record CPU samples for a running process (30 seconds):
sudo perf record -F 99 -p <PID> --sleep 30
# -F 99: sample at 99 Hz (avoids aliasing with 100Hz scheduler ticks)
# -p <PID>: target process

# View the profile:
sudo perf report                    # Interactive TUI report
sudo perf report --stdio            # Text output

# === Call graph (stack traces) ===
sudo perf record -F 99 -p <PID> -g -- sleep 30  # -g = call graphs
sudo perf report -g                 # Show call graph

# === Live profiling ===
sudo perf top                       # Real-time hottest functions (system-wide)
sudo perf top -p <PID>              # Real-time per process
sudo perf top -K                    # Kernel functions only
sudo perf top -U                    # User-space functions only

# === Static tracing ===
# Count specific events:
sudo perf stat -e cycles,instructions,cache-misses,cache-references ls
# IPC (instructions per cycle) > 1 is good; < 0.5 indicates inefficiency

# === Perf events ===
# Hardware events: cpu-cycles, instructions, cache-misses, branches, branches-misses
# Software events: context-switches, page-faults, cpu-migrations
# Tracepoint events: sched:sched_switch, block:block_rq_complete

# === perf for flamegraphs ===
sudo perf record -F 99 -a -g -- sleep 60     # System-wide call graph
sudo perf script > out.perf                  # Export for flamegraph script
# Then use Brendan Gregg's FlameGraph tools:
# git clone https://github.com/brendangregg/FlameGraph
# stackcollapse-perf.pl out.perf > out.folded
# flamegraph.pl out.folded > flamegraph.svg

# === perf diff — compare profiles ===
# After deploying a code change or config change:
sudo perf record -F 99 -p <PID> -g -o perf.before -- sleep 30
# (make change)
sudo perf record -F 99 -p <PID> -g -o perf.after -- sleep 30
perf diff perf.before perf.after     # Compare profiles

sections.title: Brendan Gregg's USE Method & Methodology
sections.description: The USE Method (Utilization, Saturation, Errors) is a systematic approach to performance analysis. For every resource (CPU, memory, disk, network), check: utilization (how busy?), saturation (how much queue?), errors (any failures?). This method, developed by Brendan Gregg, is the standard approach at Enterprise for performance investigations.
sections.codeBlock:
# === CPU ===
# Utilization:
mpstat -P ALL 1                      # Per-CPU utilization
# Saturation:
vmstat 1                             # 'r' column = run queue (processes waiting for CPU)
# Rule: r < #cores is normal; r > 2x#cores means CPU saturation
# Errors:
cat /proc/stat | grep -i "cpu"       # Look for strange counters

# === Memory ===
# Utilization:
free -h                               # Total, used, available
cat /proc/meminfo                     # Detailed breakdown
# Saturation:
vmstat 1                              # 'si' = swap in, 'so' = swap out (>0 = swapping!)
# Errors:
cat /proc/vmstat | grep -E "(oom|fail|error)"
dmesg | grep -i oom                   # OOM kills

# === Disk ===
# Utilization:
iostat -x 1                           # %util > 80% = degraded
# Saturation:
iostat -x 1                           # 'aqu-sz' > 0 = requests queued
# Errors:
iostat -x 1                           # Look at 'rrqm/s', 'wrqm/s' — merges
cat /sys/block/sda/device/state       # 'running' = ok

# === Network ===
# Utilization:
sar -n DEV 1                          # Bandwidth usage vs interface capacity
# Saturation:
ip -s link                           # 'overruns', 'dropped', 'backlog'
ss -s                                 # Socket stats
# Errors:
ip -s -s link                        # Errors, dropped, overruns
netstat -i                            # Interface error counters

# === Quick triage: 60-second checklist (Brendan Gregg) ===
# 1. uptime                             — Load averages
# 2. dmesg | tail                       — Kernel errors
# 3. vmstat 1                           — Run queue, swapping, context switches
# 4. mpstat -P ALL 1                    — Per-CPU balance
# 5. pidstat 1                          — Per-process CPU
# 6. iostat -xz 1                       — Disk I/O
# 7. free -m                            — Memory
# 8. sar -n DEV 1                       — Network
# 9. sar -n TCP,ETCP 1                  — TCP stats
# 10. top                               — Overview

sections.title: ftrace: Kernel Function Tracing
sections.description: ftrace is the kernel's built-in tracer. It can trace function calls, interrupts, scheduling, and latency events with zero overhead when disabled and very low overhead when enabled. It's available on every Linux system without installing additional tools.
sections.codeBlock:
# === ftrace basics ===
# Mount tracefs:
mount -t tracefs tracefs /sys/kernel/tracing

# === Available tracers ===
cat /sys/kernel/tracing/available_tracers
# function      — Trace function calls (high overhead)
# function_graph — Trace with call graph (nested function calls)
# preemptirqsoff — Trace preemption and IRQ-off latency
# wakeup       — Trace scheduling latency
# nop          — No tracing (default)

# === Function tracing ===
# Trace a specific function:
echo function > /sys/kernel/tracing/current_tracer
echo do_sys_open > /sys/kernel/tracing/set_ftrace_filter
echo 1 > /sys/kernel/tracing/tracing_on
# ... run your test ...
echo 0 > /sys/kernel/tracing/tracing_on
cat /sys/kernel/tracing/trace        # View trace output

# === Latency tracing ===
# Measure preemption-off latency:
echo preemptirqsoff > /sys/kernel/tracing/current_tracer
echo 0 > /sys/kernel/tracing/tracing_max_latency
# Wait for events...
cat /sys/kernel/tracing/tracing_max_latency  # Show max latency (μs)
cat /sys/kernel/tracing/trace        # Show what caused it

# === trace-cmd (front-end for ftrace) ===
sudo apt install trace-cmd
trace-cmd record -p function_graph -g do_sys_open ls
trace-cmd report                     # View recorded trace

# === wakeup latency ===
# Measures how long it takes after a task is woken up for it to actually run
echo wakeup > /sys/kernel/tracing/current_tracer
echo <PID> > /sys/kernel/tracing/set_ftrace_pid

sections.title: CPU & Memory Profiling in Practice
sections.description: When an application is slow, profiling identifies exactly where time is spent. CPU profiling samples what functions are on the CPU. Off-CPU profiling (missing in traditional profilers) shows what the process is waiting for. At Enterprise, we use both on-CPU and off-CPU analysis to understand the full picture.
sections.codeBlock:
# === On-CPU profiling ===
# Where is the CPU spending time?
perf record -F 99 -p <PID> -g -- sleep 30
perf report -g --stdio

# === Off-CPU profiling (why is the process blocked?) ===
# Use offcputime from bcc/BPF:
sudo offcputime -p <PID> 30          # Show stack traces + blocked time

# === Memory profiling ===
# /usr/bin/time -v — detailed memory usage
/usr/bin/time -v myprogram 2>&1 | grep -E "(Maximum resident|Minor|Major)"
# Maximum resident set size — peak memory
# Minor page faults — page already in cache
# Major page faults — had to read from disk (BAD for performance)

# === Cache misses ===
perf stat -e cache-misses,cache-references,LLC-loads,LLC-load-misses ./myapp
# Cache miss rate > 5% may indicate poor data locality

# === Branch mispredictions ===
perf stat -e branches,branch-misses ./myapp
# High branch miss rate (>5%) can indicate unpredictable code paths

# === IPC (Instructions Per Cycle) ===
perf stat -e cycles,instructions ./myapp
# IPC > 2: excellent (highly optimized)
# IPC 1-2: good
# IPC < 1: stalled (cache misses, branch mispredicts, memory latency)

# === Context switch profiling ===
perf stat -e context-switches,cpu-migrations,page-faults ./myapp
# High context switches: over-threaded, bad thread pool sizing
# High cpu-migrations: scheduler moving threads between CPUs (bad cache locality)

# === GDB (GNU Debugger) for performance ===
# If a process is stuck:
sudo gdb -p <PID> -batch -ex "thread apply all bt" 2>&1
# Shows all thread stack traces — great for deadlock detection

sections.title: Interview Q&A
sections.description:
Q: 'A service is using 100% CPU but throughput is low. What do you do?'

A: 'Run perf top -p to see which function is consuming CPU. This usually tells you the problem immediately. Common causes: (1) busy-waiting/polling loop, (2) inefficient algorithm (O(n²) instead of O(n log n)), (3) excessive string operations (regex, JSON parsing), (4) thread contention (spinlocks, mutex contention). If perf points to a library function, look at the call chain with perf record -g. A flamegraph makes this even clearer. At Enterprise, we run perf in staging before every major deployment to catch performance regressions.'

Q: 'How would you profile a 10-second latency spike that happens once per hour?'

A: 'This is the hardest profiling problem — an intermittent issue. Approach: (1) Set up continuous profiling (perf with rotating buffers, or BPF tools) that captures profiles and keeps them in a ring buffer. When the latency spike occurs, save the buffer. (2) Use latency tracing via ftrace to capture the longest kernel preemption-off interval during the spike. (3) Correlate with other metrics — I/O latency, network latency, GC logs. (4) The cause is often: cron job (logrotate, backup), GC pause, network retransmission, or kernel interference (kswapd, journal commit). At Enterprise, we use distributed tracing (Jaeger, Zipkin) that captures individual request latency breakdowns across services.'

Q: 'Your application uses more memory than expected. How do you find the leak?'

A: 'First, confirm it's a memory leak and not just normal growth. Run ps aux --sort=-%mem over time. Use /usr/bin/time -v to track peak RSS. For heap analysis: (1) JVM: enable -XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError and analyze the .hprof file with Eclipse MAT. (2) Node.js: use --inspect and Chrome DevTools heap snapshot. (3) Python: use tracemalloc or objgraph. (4) Native code: use Valgrind (memcheck) for precise leak detection, or perf mem for sampling. At Enterprise, memory profiling is part of the CI pipeline — we track RSS trends across builds and alert on regressions.'

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Brendan Gregg's Linux Performance — The definitive resource (tools, methodologies, talks)
  • Systems Performance: Enterprise and the Cloud — Brendan Gregg, the bible of performance analysis
  • perf Wiki — Official perf documentation and examples

learningObjectives: Understand brendan Gregg's USE Method & Methodology
Understand cPU & Memory Profiling in Practice

analogy.title: Performance as a Detective Story
analogy.description: perf is the magnifying glass. Flame graphs are the crime scene map - wide towers are suspects. USE method (Utilization, Saturation, Errors) is the investigation checklist. The culprit is usually not the CPU - it is waiting (I/O, locks, cache misses).

commonMistakes.mistake: Ignoring log rotation until disk fills up
commonMistakes.solution: Configure logrotate before deploying; monitor /var/log usage

commonMistakes.mistake: Disabling SELinux/AppArmor instead of fixing policies
commonMistakes.solution: Use `audit2allow` to generate policies from denials; keep MAC enabled
Add `USER nonroot` in Dockerfile; use `--user` flag at runtime

commonMistakes.mistake: Not setting resource limits on containers
commonMistakes.solution: Always set `--cpus` and `--memory`; configure cgroup limits in Kubernetes

practiceLabs.title: Explore: perf: The Universal Profiler
practiceLabs.task: Run `sudo` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man sudo` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.task: Run `perf` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man perf` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.hint: Use `man mpstat` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

quickReference.title: Quick Reference
quickReference.content:
| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `sudo` | perf: The Universal Profiler |
| `perf` | perf: The Universal Profiler |
| `mpstat` | Brendan Gregg's USE Method & Methodology |
| `vmstat` | Brendan Gregg's USE Method & Methodology |
| `cat` | Brendan Gregg's USE Method & Methodology |
| `mount` | ftrace: Kernel Function Tracing |
| `cat` | ftrace: Kernel Function Tracing |
| `echo` | ftrace: Kernel Function Tracing |
| `perf` | CPU & Memory Profiling in Practice |
| `sudo` | CPU & Memory Profiling in Practice |

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect arguments, wrong working directory, or stale cached data
troubleshooting.solution: Verify arguments with `man sudo`; check current path with `pwd`; clear cache if applicable

troubleshooting.cause: Service not reloaded or syntax error in config file
troubleshooting.solution: Validate config syntax with built-in test flag; reload service with `systemctl reload` or `restart`

troubleshooting.symptom: perf operation slow or timing out
troubleshooting.cause: Resource exhaustion (disk I/O, memory pressure, or lock contention)
troubleshooting.solution: Check `iostat`, `free -m`, and `top`; look for stuck processes with `ps aux --sort=-%cpu`

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect file ownership or overly restrictive permissions
troubleshooting.solution: Inspect with `ls -la`; fix with `chown`/ `chmod`; check ACLs with `getfacl`

quiz.question: What is perf: The Universal Profiler?
options: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
A tool for managing disk partitions
quiz.answer: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
quiz.explanation: The perf: The Universal Profiler is the foundation of the Linux filesystem — everything in the system is organized under this structure.

quiz.question: What does `sudo` do?
quiz.answer: Lists files and directories in the current or specified path
quiz.explanation: Use `ls` to explore directory contents. Combine with flags like `-l` (long format), `-a` (hidden files), and `-h` (human-readable sizes).

quiz.question: Which command would you use to view disk usage of the perf directory?
quiz.explanation: `du -sh` (disk usage, summary, human-readable) shows the total size of a directory and its contents.

quiz.question: A user reports "Permission denied" when trying to access a perf directory. What is the first command you should run?
quiz.explanation: `ls -ld` shows the directory's permissions and ownership. Always inspect before making changes — starting with `chmod 777` is a security anti-pattern.

quiz.question: In a perf scenario, your monitoring alerts show sudo failing intermittently. What is the correct debugging workflow?
options: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
Ignore the alert and wait for it to resolve
quiz.answer: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
quiz.explanation: A structured debugging workflow prevents cascading failures. Always diagnose before acting, especially in production environments where downtime has direct business impact.

interviewTips: FAANG Architect's Note:  Performance analysis is a superpower
FAANG, we use lightweight profilers like BPF-based tools that sample at very low rates (
productionTips: Production Tip:  Never profile in production with default sampling rates on the first try
bestPractices: Use version control for all configuration (/etc, scripts, Dockerfiles)
Implement infrastructure as code; avoid manual server changes
Test changes in staging before production; have rollback plans
enterprisePerspective: In enterprise environments, performance tuning & profiling is managed through automation (Ansible, Terraform), monitored centrally (Prometheus, Datadog), and governed by compliance policies (CIS benchmarks, SOC2). Changes go through change advisory boards with rollback procedures documented.
